hso_hl.objective: Coding practices deliver attack-surface limitation, exploitation mitigation, and AI-system attack-defence under an appropriate coding-practice evidence base.
considerations: 3 of 5 regulations participate (CRA, DORA partial, AI Act partial); GDPR and NIS 2 are explicitly out-of-scope (OJ Art. 25(1) does not specify secure-coding practices; OJ Art. 21(2)(e) does not mention CI/CD pipeline or secure-coding standards).
The coding-practice evidence base is typically built from industry frameworks (NIST SSDF + OWASP SAMM + ISO/IEC 27034) since no OJ clause prescribes specific coding standards.

The three participating regulators anchor the coding-practices duty at distinct target objects: CRA `limitation of attack surfaces, including attack surfaces of external interfaces` (Annex I Part I (2)(j)) + `reduce the impact of an incident using appropriate exploitation-mitigation mechanisms and techniques` (Annex I Part I (2)(k)) — product-coding-outcome mandate at the manufacturer side; DORA Art. 9(4)(e) change-management six-step — entity-process mandate at the financial-entity side, with implicit coding-dimension inference through `tested`/`assessed` legs + Art. 25 testing modalities (source-code reviews where feasible) — no OJ-mandated secure-coding-standards prescription (P0 disagreement on AEGIS-taxonomy sole-authority assignment); AI Act Art. 15(4)–(5) — AI-system attack-defence mandate at the AI-system-provider side (6 categories: data poisoning + model poisoning + adversarial examples + model evasion + confidentiality attacks + model flaws).

3 pairwise cells verified by CRDA-deep as 3 SAME — COMPLEMENTARY (no CORRECTED, no CONTRADICTORY), but with substantial scope-disjoint qualifiers on 2 of 3 pairs in baseline: CRA↔DORA = N typically (manufacturer vs financial-entity, product vs process); CRA↔AI_Act = Conditional Y on AI-enabled CRA products; DORA↔AI_Act = N typically (financial-entity process vs AI-system coding).

The high-level SO engages only on the Conditional-Y integrated stack (when an AI-enabled CRA product is also deployed by a DORA financial entity — the vertically-integrated manufacturer-controller-deployer stack). For a 5-FTE SaaS startup with CRA applicability only, the high-level SO is NOT activated.

The two Layer 2 review items (P0 DORA-taxonomy-disagreement + CRA outcome-level vs AI Act attack-category layered-stack requirement) are documented separately — not as CONTRADICTORY/CORRECTED reclassifications that require Phase 1C tension-resolution.

For AI-enabled CRA products, CRA Annex I Part I (2)(j) + (2)(k) outcome-level obligations + AI Act Art. 15(4)–(5) attack-defence categories stack with **no OJ-level consolidation rule** (unlike CRA Art. 13(4) sentence 2 unified-risk-assessment carve-out for AI Act Art. 9, which does not extend to coding practices).

The per-reg perspective (target object, trigger, anchor, threshold, evidence template, recipient, legal anchor) is preserved verbatim in the three sub-SOs; the per-tier scope-disjoint structure is surfaced as a 2-of-3 N qualifier in baseline.
hso_hl.activation: auto  # activates when ≥2 applicable regs have ≥1 pair with Y/Conditional Y scope overlap on the same party AND ≥1 sub-SO activates; for D-07.2 only 1 of 3 pairs (CRA↔AI_Act) has Conditional Y scope overlap on the AI-enabled CRA product integrated stack; the other 2 pairs (CRA↔DORA + DORA↔AI_Act) are N in baseline — so the high-level SO engages ONLY on the Conditional Y integrated stack (AI-enabled CRA product deployed by a DORA financial entity or similar integrated stack). For a 5-FTE SaaS startup with CRA applicability only, the high-level SO is NOT activated.

hso_per_reg.objective: The manufacturer designs, develops, and produces the product with digital elements to limit its attack surfaces, including attack surfaces of external interfaces (Annex I Part I (2)(j) CRA), and to reduce the impact of an incident using appropriate exploitation-mitigation mechanisms and techniques (Annex I Part I (2)(k) CRA).
considerations: `attack-surface limitation` is the outcome-level mandate on product-side coding — minimum-necessary external interfaces, minimum-necessary attack surfaces, and minimum-necessary exploitable paths.

`exploitation-mitigation` is the outcome-level mandate on product-side incident-impact-reduction — appropriate mechanisms and techniques to reduce the impact of an incident (ASLR, stack-canaries, control-flow integrity, sandboxing, memory-safe language subsystems, bounds checking, least-privilege subsystems, reduced-privilege components as typical mechanisms).

The CRA-side participation at D-07.2 is partial in the SR sense: OJ Annex I Part I (2)(j) + (2)(k) prescribes outcome-level mandates but does NOT specify secure-coding standards (no coding-language-specific linting, no secure-coding-framework adoption, no NIST SSDF/OWASP SAMM/ISO/IEC 27034 enforcement). The manufacturer builds the coding-standards baseline from industry frameworks (NIST SSDF, OWASP SAMM, ISO/IEC 27034) and demonstrates compliance against the Annex I Part I (2)(j) + (2)(k) outcome-level obligations.
CRA-side coding-outcomes are demonstrated in Annex VII §2 design/development documentation + Annex VII §3 risk-assessment record (cross to D-09.4 — SO-CRA-059 + SO-CRA-053).

The Annex I Part I (2) attack-surface + exploitation-mitigation outcomes are propagated downstream from the 6-phase CRA risk-assessment-propagation at D-07.1 (planning + design + development + production + delivery + maintenance) — at the development phase, the coding-outcome decisions are realised through in-house secure-coding decisions.

For an AI-enabled CRA product, CRA-side Annex I Part I (2)(j) + (2)(k) outcome mandate stacks with AI Act Art. 15(4)–(5) AI-attack-defence mandate — the two regimes DO NOT substitute; **no OJ-level consolidation rule exists for the coding-practices dimension** (unlike CRA Art. 13(4) sentence 2 unified-risk-assessment carve-out for AI Act Art. 9, which does not extend to coding practices).

For a 5-FTE manufacturer of a CRA-regulated product, the coding-outcomes baseline is built from industry frameworks and demonstrated against Annex I Part I (2)(j) + (2)(k) outcome-level obligations via the Annex VII §2 design/development documentation record.

hso_per_reg.inherits_from: SO-DORA-013 (partial via Art. 9(4)(e) — D-07.4 primary, D-07.2 partial by inference only)
hso_per_reg.objective: Financial entities implement documented policies, procedures and controls for ICT change management, including changes to software, hardware, firmware components, systems or security parameters, that are based on a risk assessment approach, in order to ensure that all changes to ICT systems are recorded, tested, assessed, approved, implemented and verified in a controlled manner (Art. 9(4)(e) DORA — 6-step change-management process).
considerations: The secure-coding dimension for DORA is implicit through the `tested` and `assessed` legs of the change-mgmt six-step + the Art. 25 testing modalities (12-way open list including vulnerability assessments, scans, source code reviews where feasible, scenario-based tests, penetration testing, etc.).

The DORA-side participation at D-07.2 is partial in the SR sense: no OJ mandate prescribes secure-coding standards specifically. The DORA-side coding dimension is derived from the change-mgmt six-step's `tested` + `assessed` legs + the Art. 25 testing modalities (source-code reviews where feasible as one of 12 modalities).

**P0 disagreement ON PARTICIPATION (preserved as Layer 2 review item):** AEGIS taxonomy §4.1 marks DORA as SOLE AUTHORITY for D-07.2 but this assignment has no OJ-text anchor per CRDA-deep verification (Art. 9(4)(e) is change-management policy; Art. 9(4)(f) is patches-and-updates policy; no OJ clause prescribes secure-coding standards). Either (a) revise the taxonomy to remove the DORA sole-authority assignment, or (b) identify the OJ clause that supports it.

For a 5-FTE financial entity, the DORA-side coding dimension is demonstrated through: (1) documented Art. 9(4)(e) change-management policies/procedures/controls with the 6-step process; (2) Art. 25 testing programme with the 12-way open testing-modality list; (3) Art. 11(3) response-and-recovery plans + Art. 11(2) backup-policies — NOT through an OJ-mandated secure-coding-standards prescription.

The DORA-side coding-output is the change-management-evidenced deployed code; the coding-standards baseline itself (NIST SSDF/OWASP SAMM/ISO/IEC 27034) is supplied by the entity's ICT risk-management-policy tier rather than by an OJ mandate.

CRDA-deep substantive note: the `complementary` label for CRA↔DORA is more a taxonomy artifact than a substantive relationship — the two regimes do not overlap substantively on coding practices (CRA covers the product, DORA covers the process); the high-level SO does NOT engage on the CRA↔DORA pair in baseline because the pair is substantively scope-disjoint.

hso_per_reg.objective: High-risk AI systems are designed and developed with appropriate technical and organisational measures to enable the AI systems to effectively resist, as appropriate to the AI system's purpose and conditions of use, attempts to manipulate the training data set (data poisoning) or pre-trained components used in training (model poisoning), inputs designed to cause the AI model to make a mistake (adversarial examples or model evasion), confidentiality attacks or model flaws (Art. 15(4)–(5) AI Act).
considerations: The 6 AI-attack-defence categories are: (1) data poisoning — manipulation of the training data set; (2) model poisoning — manipulation of pre-trained components; (3) adversarial examples — inputs designed to cause the AI model to make a mistake; (4) model evasion — adversarial-example-induced evasion; (5) confidentiality attacks — attacks on confidentiality of training data, parameters, or outputs; (6) model flaws — vulnerabilities or weaknesses inherent in model architecture or training process.

The `as appropriate to the AI system's purpose and conditions of use` is a proportionality qualifier at the AI-system-purpose level — calibration is informed by the intended purpose (cross to D-09.2 — SO-AIACT-001 Art. 9 risk-management-system) and the conditions of use (operational environment + reasonably foreseeable use).

The AI-Act-side participation at D-07.2 is partial in the SR sense: Art. 15(4)–(5) AI-attack-defence categories are AI/ML-specific and do NOT map to conventional secure-coding mandates (no general coding-standards prescription in AI Act — the AI Act addresses AI-attack-defence, not conventional coding practices).

For an AI-enabled CRA product, AI Act Art. 15(4)–(5) AI-attack-defence stacks with CRA Annex I Part I (2)(j) + (2)(k) attack-surface + exploitation-mitigation — the two regimes DO NOT substitute (AI Act defence categories do not substitute for CRA's outcome-level obligations, and vice versa); **no OJ-level consolidation rule exists for the coding-practices dimension** (unlike CRA Art. 13(4) sentence 2 unified-risk-assessment carve-out for AI Act Art. 9, which does not extend to coding practices).

AI-Act-side attack-defence is documented in QMS design-control records (Art. 17(1)(c) design/design-control/design-verification) + Annex IV technical documentation + Art. 9 risk-management-system documentation (cross to D-09.1 — SO-AIACT-010 + SO-AIACT-001).

For a high-risk AI system provider, the AI-attack-defence coding-practices baseline is built from AI/ML-specific frameworks (NIST AI RMF, ISO/IEC 23894, MITRE ATLAS) and demonstrated against the 6 Art. 15(4)–(5) attack-defence categories via the QMS + Annex IV documentation.

AI-Act-Recital 78 GDPR preservation operates at the AI-system side for high-risk AI systems processing personal data — the AI-Act-side AI-attack-defence does NOT substitute for the GDPR Art. 25(1) data-protection by-design; the GDPR-side by-design is layered on top via the controller-means-determination + processing-time by-default.

## 1. Cross-Regulation Analysis [VERBATIM]


> **Domain analysis:** [D-07.2 Secure Coding Practices](../../CrossRegulation/DomainAnalysis/D-07_Secure-Development/D-07.2.md)

### D-07.2 Secure Coding Practices

<!-- participants: CRA, DORA, AI_Act -->

**Participants (from CRDA):** CRA, DORA, AI_Act

**Sole-authority note (CRDA flag):** AEGIS taxonomy §4.1 marks DORA as sole authority for D-07.2 Secure Coding Practices. The OJ Art. 9(4)(f) text is on `patches and updates` (patch-management policy) — not coding standards. Per SR-DORA-007 v0.2 audit, this pilot finds no OJ-text support for the DORA sole-authority assignment and recommends Layer 2 review (see P0 disagreement report).

#### Pair: CRA ↔ DORA

**CRA article (verbatim, Annex I Part I (2)(j) + (2)(k)):**
> "On the basis of the cybersecurity risk assessment referred to in Article 13(2) … the product with digital elements shall: … (j) limit its attack surfaces, including attack surfaces of external interfaces … (k) reduce the impact of an incident using appropriate exploitation mitigation mechanisms and techniques." — CRA Annex I Part I (2)(j)+(k)

**DORA article (verbatim, Art. 9(4)(e)):**
> [as quoted in NIS2 ↔ DORA above]

**Scope comparison:**
- CRA scope: product-level outcome mandates (limit attack surfaces, exploit mitigation).
- DORA scope: financial-entity ICT change-management six-step (recorded/tested/assessed/approved/implemented/verified).
- Scope overlap: **N (typically)** — CRA addresses product-level coding outcomes; DORA addresses entity-level process.

**Trigger comparison:**
- CRA trigger: product design / development (Annex I Part I chapeau).
- DORA trigger: ICT change event (software/hardware/firmware/system/security parameter change).
- Trigger alignment: **DIFFERENT** (product design vs. entity change).

**Threshold/timeline comparison:**
- CRA: no quantitative threshold; Annex I (2)(j) `(k)` qualitative.
- DORA: no quantitative cadence; six-step mandatory.
- Tension: **NONE (scope-disjoint)**.

**Recipient comparison:**
- CRA: MSA.
- DORA: NCA, management body.

**Content template comparison:**
- CRA: Annex VII §2 design/development documentation + risk-assessment record (§3).
- DORA: documented change-management policy.

**Classified relationship (from CRDA):** COMPLEMENTARY
**Verified relationship (after OJ-text analysis):** **SAME — COMPLEMENTARY** (CRDA classification preserved; but see P0 disagreement on DORA OJ-text support for D-07.2)
**Reasoning:** CRA Annex I Part I (2)(j)+(k) specifies product-level outcomes (attack-surface reduction, exploitation mitigation). DORA Art. 9(4)(e) specifies entity-level process (change-management six-step) but does not prescribe coding standards. The two regimes do not overlap substantively on coding practices — CRA covers the product, DORA covers the process. No DORA SR exists for D-07.2 from OJ-literal text (the SR-DORA-007 audit confirms Art. 9(4)(f) is patch-management policy, not coding standards).

**Scope-disjoint test:** Does this pair actually overlap on a single event at a single party? **N (typically)** — different objects (product coding outcomes vs. entity change process), different actors (manufacturer vs. financial entity). Pair is scope-disjoint in the OJ-literal reading; CRDA's "complementary" label is more a taxonomy artifact than a substantive relationship.

**Downstream implication (Phase 1C Doc 07):** Phase 1 entities must NOT treat DORA as a coding-standards authority absent OJ-text evidence (see P0 disagreement). Coding-standards baseline must be built from industry frameworks (NIST SSDF, OWASP SAMM, ISO 27034) and demonstrated against each regulation's outcome (CRA) or process (DORA) requirement.

---

#### Pair: CRA ↔ AI_Act

**CRA article (verbatim, Annex I Part I (2)(j) + (2)(k)):**
> [as quoted above]

**AI_Act article (verbatim, Art. 15(4)–(5)):**
> "High-risk AI systems shall … be designed and developed with appropriate technical and organisational measures to … (a) … (b) enable the AI systems to effectively resist, as appropriate, to the AI system's purpose and conditions of use, attempts to manipulate the training data set (data poisoning) or pre-trained components used in training (model poisoning), inputs designed to cause the AI model to make a mistake (adversarial examples or model evasion), confidentiality attacks or model flaws." — Art. 15(4) AI Act (per the SR-AIACT-016 reading)

**Scope comparison:**
- CRA scope: product-level coding outcomes (attack-surface reduction, exploit mitigation) — generic product coding.
- AI Act scope: AI/ML-specific attack defence (data poisoning, model poisoning, adversarial examples, model evasion, confidentiality attacks, model flaws).
- Scope overlap: **Conditional (Y when the CRA-regulated product is an AI-enabled product with high-risk AI components).**

**Trigger comparison:**
- CRA trigger: product design/development.
- AI Act trigger: high-risk AI system design/development.
- Trigger alignment: **DIFFERENT** (generic product vs. AI-specific).

**Threshold/timeline comparison:**
- CRA: qualitative.
- AI Act: qualitative.
- Tension: **NONE**.

**Recipient comparison:**
- CRA: MSA.
- AI Act: MSA, AI Office.

**Content template comparison:**
- CRA: Annex VII §2 design/development documentation.
- AI Act: QMS design-control + Annex IV + Art. 15(4)/(5) attack-defence measures.

**Classified relationship (from CRDA):** COMPLEMENTARY
**Verified relationship (after OJ-text analysis):** **SAME — COMPLEMENTARY**
**Reasoning:** CRA covers conventional product coding practices (attack-surface, exploitation); AI Act covers AI/ML-specific attacks (data/model poisoning, adversarial examples, model evasion, confidentiality attacks, model flaws) per SR-AIACT-016. For AI-enabled products, both apply — CRA Annex I (2)(j)/(k) plus AI Act Art. 15(4)-(5) — but the AI Act defence categories do not substitute for CRA's outcome-level obligations.

**Scope-disjoint test:** Does this pair actually overlap on a single event at a single party? **Conditional (Y for AI-enabled high-risk products; N for CRA-regulated products without AI)**. No reclassification required.

**Downstream implication (Phase 1C Doc 07):** Phase 2 obligation derivation must produce a layered coding-defence stack for AI-enabled CRA products: CRA Annex I (2)(j)/(k) generic coding + AI Act Art. 15(4)/(5) AI-specific attack-defence.

---

#### Pair: DORA ↔ AI_Act

**DORA article (verbatim, Art. 9(4)(e)):**
> [as quoted above]

**AI_Act article (verbatim, Art. 15(4)–(5)):**
> [as quoted above]

**Scope comparison:**
- DORA scope: entity-level ICT change-management (no OJ mandate for coding standards — see P0 disagreement).
- AI Act scope: AI-system attack-defence.
- Scope overlap: **N (typically)** — different objects (entity process vs. AI-system coding).

**Trigger comparison:**
- DORA trigger: ICT change event.
- AI Act trigger: high-risk AI system design/development.
- Trigger alignment: **DIFFERENT**.

**Threshold/timeline comparison:**
- DORA: no quantitative threshold.
- AI Act: no quantitative threshold.
- Tension: **NONE**.

**Recipient comparison:**
- DORA: NCA, management body.
- AI Act: MSA, AI Office.

**Content template comparison:**
- DORA: change-management policy.
- AI Act: QMS design-control + Art. 15(4)/(5) attack-defence.

**Classified relationship (from CRDA):** COMPLEMENTARY
**Verified relationship (after OJ-text analysis):** **SAME — COMPLEMENTARY** (but with scope-disjoint qualifier per OJ-literal reading)
**Reasoning:** No OJ-level overlap. AI Act addresses AI/ML coding (Art. 15(4)/(5)); DORA addresses process change-management (Art. 9(4)(e)). The "sole authority" taxonomy assignment for DORA at D-07.2 is a taxonomy artifact that requires Layer 2 review (no OJ mandate for coding standards in DORA). When a DORA financial entity deploys high-risk AI, both regimes apply at different layers: DORA change-management gate + AI Act AI-specific attack-defence.

**Scope-disjoint test:** Does this pair actually overlap on a single event at a single party? **N (typically)** — different objects, different actors, different lifecycle stages. Pair is substantively scope-disjoint; CRDA "complementary" is a taxonomy artifact.

**Downstream implication (Phase 1C Doc 07):** Phase 2 obligation derivation must produce two parallel tracks: DORA change-management (Art. 9(4)(e)) + AI Act attack-defence (Art. 15(4)/(5)). No OJ-level consolidation rule exists for coding practices (unlike CRA Art. 13(4) sentence 2 AI-Act-substitution for risk assessment, which does not extend to coding).

- **CRA ↔ AI_Act** — COMPLEMENTARY (SAME): CRA covers **conventional product coding practices** (attack-surface, exploitation); AI Act covers **AI/ML-specific attacks** (data poisoning, model poisoning, adversarial examples, model evasion, confidentiality attacks, model flaws) per SR-AIACT-016. For an **AI-enabled CRA product** (CRA-regulated product that embeds a high-risk AI system), both regimes apply — CRA Annex I (2)(j) + (2)(k) plus AI Act Art. 15(4)–(5) — but the AI Act defence categories do not substitute for CRA's outcome-level obligations. The scope-disjoint test: **Conditional (Y when the CRA-regulated product is an AI-enabled product with high-risk AI components; N for CRA-regulated products without AI)**.
- **DORA ↔ AI_Act** — COMPLEMENTARY (SAME) (CRDA classification preserved; **but with scope-disjoint qualifier per OJ-literal reading** — CRDA-deep notes the 'sole authority' taxonomy assignment for DORA at D-07.2 is a taxonomy artifact that requires Layer 2 review; **no OJ mandate for coding standards in DORA**): No OJ-level overlap. AI Act addresses AI/ML coding (Art. 15(4)–(5)); DORA addresses process change-management (Art. 9(4)(e)). The "sole authority" taxonomy assignment for DORA at D-07.2 is a taxonomy artifact that requires Layer 2 review — no OJ mandate for coding standards in DORA. When a DORA financial entity deploys high-risk AI, both regimes apply at different layers: **DORA change-management gate (Art. 9(4)(e)) + AI Act AI-specific attack-defence (Art. 15(4)–(5))**. The scope-disjoint test: **N (typically)** — different objects (entity process vs AI-system coding), different actors, different lifecycle stages. Pair is substantively scope-disjoint; CRDA "complementary" is a taxonomy artifact.

The secure-coding architecture is **legitimately diverse with substantial scope-disjoint structure** — each regulator targets a **distinct target object** (product-level coding outcomes [attack-surface + exploitation] / entity-level process [change-mgmt six-step with implicit coding-dimension inference — no OJ mandate] / AI-system-level attack-defence [6 AI/ML-specific categories]) at a **distinct trigger** (manufacturer places product on market [Annex I Part I chapeau] / financial entity ICT change event [Art. 9(4)(e)] / high-risk AI system design/development [Art. 15(4)–(5)]) with a **distinct obligation anchor** (CRA `attack-surface limitation + exploitation-mitigation` outcome mandate / DORA `recorded/tested/assessed/approved/implemented/verified` change-mgmt process mandate with implicit coding-inference dimension / AI Act 6-category AI-attack-defence mandate). The high-level SO surfaces the **common secure-coding duty** (attack-surface limitation + exploitation mitigation + AI-attack-defence + appropriate coding-practice evidence base — typically built from industry frameworks NIST SSDF + OWASP SAMM + ISO/IEC 27034 since no OJ clause prescribes specific coding standards) while preserving the **per-reg target object** and acknowledging the **substantial scope-disjoint structure on 2 of 3 pairs in baseline**. The high-level SO does **NOT** engage on the baseline 2-of-3 N scope-disjoint pairs in the typical integrated stack; it engages only on the Conditional-Y integrated stack (when an AI-enabled CRA product is also deployed by a DORA financial entity — the vertically-integrated manufacturer-controller-deployer stack).

The scope-overlap column in the matrix below indicates whether the same event can trigger both regulations on a single party; this is the gating condition for the high-level SO to engage.

|---|---|---|---|
| **CRA** | — | N (typically) — CRA is product-side coding outcomes (manufacturer); DORA is entity-side process change-management (financial entity); distinct actors, distinct objects, distinct supply-chain levels — scope-disjoint in baseline. CRDA-deep scope-disjoint tests: **CRA↔DORA = N (typically)** (substantive scope-disjoint because distinct actors handle distinct objects — manufacturer-side product coding outcomes vs financial-entity-side process change-management); **CRA↔AI_Act = Conditional Y** (Y for AI-enabled high-risk CRA products; N for CRA-regulated products without AI); **DORA↔AI_Act = N (typically)** (substantive scope-disjoint because distinct actors handle distinct objects — financial-entity-side process change-management vs AI-system-coding). **The high-level SO engages only on the Conditional-Y integrated stacks**; on the 2-of-3 N scope-disjoint baseline pairs, the high-level SO does **NOT** engage in baseline.

**Objective.** The manufacturer designs, develops, and produces the product with digital elements to limit its attack surfaces, including attack surfaces of external interfaces (Annex I Part I (2)(j) CRA), and to reduce the impact of an incident using appropriate exploitation-mitigation mechanisms and techniques (Annex I Part I (2)(k) CRA).

**Considerations.**
- `attack-surface limitation` is the outcome-level mandate on product-side coding — minimum-necessary external interfaces, minimum-necessary attack surfaces, and minimum-necessary exploitable paths.
- `exploitation-mitigation` is the outcome-level mandate on product-side incident-impact-reduction — appropriate mechanisms and techniques to reduce the impact of an incident (ASLR, stack-canaries, control-flow integrity, sandboxing, memory-safe language subsystems, bounds checking, least-privilege subsystems, reduced-privilege components as typical mechanisms).
- The CRA-side participation at D-07.2 is partial in the SR sense: OJ Annex I Part I (2)(j) + (2)(k) prescribes outcome-level mandates but does NOT specify secure-coding standards (no coding-language-specific linting, no secure-coding-framework adoption, no NIST SSDF/OWASP SAMM/ISO/IEC 27034 enforcement). The manufacturer builds the coding-standards baseline from industry frameworks (NIST SSDF, OWASP SAMM, ISO/IEC 27034) and demonstrates compliance against the Annex I Part I (2)(j) + (2)(k) outcome-level obligations.
- CRA-side coding-outcomes are demonstrated in Annex VII §2 design/development documentation + Annex VII §3 risk-assessment record (cross to D-09.4 — SO-CRA-059 + SO-CRA-053).
- The Annex I Part I (2) attack-surface + exploitation-mitigation outcomes are propagated downstream from the 6-phase CRA risk-assessment-propagation at D-07.1 (planning + design + development + production + delivery + maintenance) — at the development phase, the coding-outcome decisions are realised through in-house secure-coding decisions.
- For an AI-enabled CRA product, CRA-side Annex I Part I (2)(j) + (2)(k) outcome mandate stacks with AI Act Art. 15(4)–(5) AI-attack-defence mandate — the two regimes DO NOT substitute; **no OJ-level consolidation rule exists for the coding-practices dimension** (unlike CRA Art. 13(4) sentence 2 unified-risk-assessment carve-out for AI Act Art. 9, which does not extend to coding practices).
- For a 5-FTE manufacturer of a CRA-regulated product, the coding-outcomes baseline is built from industry frameworks and demonstrated against Annex I Part I (2)(j) + (2)(k) outcome-level obligations via the Annex VII §2 design/development documentation record.

*(PR.PS-06 anchors the secure-software-development lifecycle dimension — secure software development practices are integrated, and their performance is monitored throughout the SDLC; the CRA Annex I Part I (2)(j) + (2)(k) attack-surface + exploitation-mitigation outcomes are realised through the SDLC, with the manufacturer building the coding-standards baseline from industry frameworks. PR.PS-02 anchors the software-maintenance dimension — software is maintained, replaced, and removed commensurate with risk; the Annex I Part I (2)(j) + (2)(k) attack-surface + exploitation-mitigation outcomes are maintained across the support period ≥ 5 years (Art. 13(8) sentence 3) — with ongoing exploitation-mitigation updates + attack-surface monitoring + vulnerability remediation per Art. 13(6) (cross to D-02.2 — SO-CRA-008 + SO-CRA-010). The two subcategories together enable the manufacturer to demonstrate ex post, through the documented Annex VII §2 design/development documentation record + Annex VII §3 risk-assessment record + Annex I Part I (2)(j)/(k) outcome evidence + Art. 13(8) support-period record, that the attack-surface limitation + exploitation-mitigation coding-outcome duties have been exercised with the Annex I Part I essential-requirements anchor + 6-phase risk-assessment propagation.)*

**Objective.** High-risk AI systems are designed and developed with appropriate technical and organisational measures to enable the AI systems to effectively resist, as appropriate to the AI system's purpose and conditions of use, attempts to manipulate the training data set (data poisoning) or pre-trained components used in training (model poisoning), inputs designed to cause the AI model to make a mistake (adversarial examples or model evasion), confidentiality attacks or model flaws (Art. 15(4)–(5) AI Act).

**Considerations.**
- The 6 AI-attack-defence categories are: (1) data poisoning — manipulation of the training data set; (2) model poisoning — manipulation of pre-trained components; (3) adversarial examples — inputs designed to cause the AI model to make a mistake; (4) model evasion — adversarial-example-induced evasion; (5) confidentiality attacks — attacks on confidentiality of training data, parameters, or outputs; (6) model flaws — vulnerabilities or weaknesses inherent in model architecture or training process.
- The `as appropriate to the AI system's purpose and conditions of use` is a proportionality qualifier at the AI-system-purpose level — calibration is informed by the intended purpose (cross to D-09.2 — SO-AIACT-001 Art. 9 risk-management-system) and the conditions of use (operational environment + reasonably foreseeable use).
- The AI-Act-side participation at D-07.2 is partial in the SR sense: Art. 15(4)–(5) AI-attack-defence categories are AI/ML-specific and do NOT map to conventional secure-coding mandates (no general coding-standards prescription in AI Act — the AI Act addresses AI-attack-defence, not conventional coding practices).
- For an AI-enabled CRA product, AI Act Art. 15(4)–(5) AI-attack-defence stacks with CRA Annex I Part I (2)(j) + (2)(k) attack-surface + exploitation-mitigation — the two regimes DO NOT substitute (AI Act defence categories do not substitute for CRA's outcome-level obligations, and vice versa); **no OJ-level consolidation rule exists for the coding-practices dimension** (unlike CRA Art. 13(4) sentence 2 unified-risk-assessment carve-out for AI Act Art. 9, which does not extend to coding practices).
- AI-Act-side attack-defence is documented in QMS design-control records (Art. 17(1)(c) design/design-control/design-verification) + Annex IV technical documentation + Art. 9 risk-management-system documentation (cross to D-09.1 — SO-AIACT-010 + SO-AIACT-001).
- For a high-risk AI system provider, the AI-attack-defence coding-practices baseline is built from AI/ML-specific frameworks (NIST AI RMF, ISO/IEC 23894, MITRE ATLAS) and demonstrated against the 6 Art. 15(4)–(5) attack-defence categories via the QMS + Annex IV documentation.
- AI-Act-Recital 78 GDPR preservation operates at the AI-system side for high-risk AI systems processing personal data — the AI-Act-side AI-attack-defence does NOT substitute for the GDPR Art. 25(1) data-protection by-design; the GDPR-side by-design is layered on top via the controller-means-determination + processing-time by-default.

### 7.2.1 — Product coding outcomes: attack-surface limitation + exploitation-mitigation

```yaml
- req_id: 7.2.1
  volere_section: 15
  volere_subtype: immunity

  title: "Product with digital elements designed, developed and produced to limit attack surfaces (including external interfaces) and to reduce incident impact via exploitation-mitigation mechanisms and techniques"

  derives_from:
    hso_sub_so: SO-D-07.2.CRA
    per_reg_so: SO-CRA-046
    source_sr: SR-CRA-067, SR-CRA-068

    objective_ref_section: "### D-07.2.1"

  description: |
    The product shall be designed, developed and produced to limit its attack surfaces — including attack surfaces of external interfaces — and to reduce the impact of an incident using appropriate exploitation-mitigation mechanisms and techniques (CRA Annex I Part I (2)(j) + Annex I Part I (2)(k) + Art. 13(1) + Art. 13(2) risk-assessment propagation across 6 lifecycle phases).

  fit_criterion: |
    A documented attack-surface-limitation discipline is maintained per Annex I Part I (2)(j) — minimum-necessary external interfaces (default-closed port suppression, surface-area-baselining), minimum-necessary attack surfaces, minimum-necessary exploitable paths; configuration-management practices are established, documented, and applied to assets per PR.PS-01 with default-closed external interfaces and surface-area-baselining; software is maintained commensurate with risk per PR.PS-02 with risk-assessment-driven attack-surface reduction per Art. 13(2) 6-phase risk-assessment propagation; a documented exploitation-mitigation discipline is maintained per Annex I Part I (2)(k) — appropriate mechanisms and techniques to reduce the impact of an incident (ASLR, DEP/NX, stack canaries, control-flow integrity / CFI, sandboxing, secure enclaves, configuration hardening, principle of least privilege at process level) commensurate with risk per Art. 13(2) risk assessment; the SDLC-process evidence in the technical documentation under Annex VII §2 shows which mitigation mechanisms are in scope for the product and how they are configured; Annex VII §3 risk-assessment record documents the attack-surface + exploitation-mitigation risk-assessment outputs; the coding-practices evidence base is built from industry frameworks (NIST SSDF + OWASP SAMM + ISO/IEC 27034) since OJ Annex I Part I (2)(j) + (2)(k) does NOT specify secure-coding standards (no coding-language-specific linting, no secure-coding-framework adoption prescription); the support period ≥ 5 years per Art. 13(8) sentence 3 is maintained with ongoing exploitation-mitigation updates + attack-surface monitoring + vulnerability remediation per Art. 13(6); for AI-enabled CRA products, CRA Annex I Part I (2)(j) + (2)(k) outcome-level obligations stack with AI Act Art. 15(4)–(5) attack-defence categories — **no OJ-level consolidation rule** exists for coding practices (unlike CRA Art. 13(4) sentence 2 unified-risk-assessment carve-out for AI Act Art. 9 risk assessment, which does NOT extend to coding practices); verified by Annex I Part I (2)(j) attack-surface-limitation evidence audit (default-closed interfaces, surface-area baseline) + Annex I Part I (2)(k) exploitation-mitigation mechanism inventory + Annex VII §2 design/development documentation review + Annex VII §3 risk-assessment record audit + 6-phase risk-assessment-propagation records inspection + Art. 13(6) vulnerability remediation records + Art. 13(8) support-period record + MSA / ENISA inspection-ready evidence pack on request.

  priority: MUST
  verification_method: TEST
  dependencies: [7.2.3 (when AI-enabled CRA product — CRA Annex I Part I (2)(j)+(k) outcome-level obligations + AI Act Art. 15(4)–(5) AI-attack-defence categories stack vertically with no OJ-level consolidation rule)]

  nist_csf: [PR.PS-06, PR.PS-02]

  applicable_if:
    regs: [CRA]
    scope_overlap: Conditional   # CRA↔DORA = N typically (manufacturer vs financial-entity; product vs process; scope-disjoint in baseline); CRA↔AI_Act = Conditional Y on AI-enabled CRA products; HL engages only on the Conditional Y integrated stack

  satisfaction:
    controls: []
    nodes: []

  considerations: |
    The CRA secure-coding obligation is the product-level coding-outcome mandate at the manufacturer side; the operational substrate is the Annex I Part I (2)(j) attack-surface-limitation discipline (mapped to PR.PS-01 configuration-management + PR.PS-02 software maintenance) + the Annex I Part I (2)(k) exploitation-mitigation discipline (mapped to PR.PS-02 + PR.PS-06 SDLC). The two complementary outcome mandates operate together: (j) reduces what an attacker can reach; (k) reduces what the attacker can do once reached. The CRA-side participation at D-07.2 is **partial** in the SR sense: OJ Annex I Part I (2)(j) + (2)(k) prescribes outcome-level mandates but does NOT specify secure-coding standards (no coding-language-specific linting, no secure-coding-framework adoption, no NIST SSDF/OWASP SAMM/ISO/IEC 27034 enforcement). The manufacturer builds the coding-standards baseline from industry frameworks and demonstrates compliance against the Annex I Part I (2)(j) + (2)(k) outcome-level obligations. For AI-enabled CRA products, CRA-side outcome-level obligations stack with AI Act Art. 15(4)–(5) AI-attack-defence categories with **no OJ-level consolidation rule** (unlike CRA Art. 13(4) sentence 2 unified-risk-assessment carve-out for AI Act Art. 9 risk assessment, which does NOT extend to coding practices). The `complementary` CRDA classification label for CRA↔DORA is more a taxonomy artifact than a substantive relationship — the two regimes do not overlap substantively on coding practices (CRA covers the product, DORA covers the process); the pair is substantively scope-disjoint in baseline. (Copied from the HSO Considerations block of `### D-07.2.1`; preserved here for self-contained traceability.)
```

### 7.2.3 — High-risk AI system attack-defence across 6 AI/ML-specific categories

```yaml
- req_id: 7.2.3
  volere_section: 15
  volere_subtype: immunity

  title: "High-risk AI systems designed and developed to effectively resist, as appropriate to purpose and conditions of use, attempts at training-data poisoning, model poisoning, adversarial examples, model evasion, confidentiality attacks or model flaws"

  derives_from:
    hso_sub_so: SO-D-07.2.AIACT
    per_reg_so: SO-AIACT-009 (partial via Art. 15(4)–(5))
    source_sr: SR-AIACT-016
    hso_file: ../../HierarchicalSecurityObjectives/D-07_Secure-Development/D-07.2.md
    objective_ref_section: "### D-07.2.3"

  description: |
    The product shall ensure that high-risk AI systems are designed and developed with appropriate technical and organisational measures to enable the AI systems to effectively resist, as appropriate to the AI system's purpose and conditions of use, attempts to manipulate the training data set (data poisoning) or pre-trained components used in training (model poisoning), inputs designed to cause the AI model to make a mistake (adversarial examples or model evasion), confidentiality attacks or model flaws (AI Act Art. 15(4)–(5)).

  fit_criterion: |
    A documented Art. 15(4) attack-defence baseline is maintained for the 6 AI/ML-specific attack categories: (1) **data poisoning** — defence against manipulation of the training data set, including input-validation + data-provenance checks + training-data sanitisation + anomaly-detection on training-set distribution; (2) **model poisoning** — defence against manipulation of pre-trained components, including supply-chain integrity for pre-trained components + signed-model checksums + pre-training-component provenance verification; (3) **adversarial examples** — defence against inputs designed to cause the AI model to make a mistake, including adversarial-input detection + input-preprocessing hardening + adversarial-robustness training; (4) **model evasion** — defence against adversarial-example-induced evasion, including evasion-aware model-update cadence + detection-of-evasion-attempt signals; (5) **confidentiality attacks** — defence against attacks on confidentiality of training data, parameters, or outputs, including differential-privacy training + model-parameter access controls + output-redaction discipline + membership-inference resistance; (6) **model flaws** — defence against vulnerabilities or weaknesses inherent in model architecture or training process, including architecture-review discipline + known-flaw catalogue + flaw-driven model-update cadence; the baseline is calibrated to `appropriate to the AI system's purpose and conditions of use` (Art. 15(4) proportionality qualifier) — informed by intended purpose (cross to D-09.2 — SO-AIACT-001 Art. 9 risk-management-system) and conditions of use (operational environment + reasonably foreseeable use); the Art. 15(4)–(5) AI-attack-defence categories are AI/ML-specific and do NOT map to conventional secure-coding mandates (no general coding-standards prescription in AI Act — the AI Act addresses AI-attack-defence, not conventional coding practices); the AI-attack-defence coding-practices baseline is built from AI/ML-specific frameworks (NIST AI RMF, ISO/IEC 23894, MITRE ATLAS) and demonstrated against the 6 Art. 15(4)–(5) attack-defence categories via the QMS + Annex IV documentation; AI-Act-Recital 78 GDPR preservation operates at the AI-system side for high-risk AI systems processing personal data — the AI-Act-side AI-attack-defence does NOT substitute for the GDPR Art. 25(1) data-protection by-design; the GDPR-side by-design is layered on top via the controller-means-determination + processing-time by-default; for AI-enabled CRA products (CRA-regulated product that embeds a high-risk AI system), AI Act Art. 15(4)–(5) AI-attack-defence stacks with CRA Annex I Part I (2)(j) attack-surface + (2)(k) exploitation-mitigation — the two regimes DO NOT substitute (AI Act defence categories do not substitute for CRA's outcome-level obligations, and vice versa); **no OJ-level consolidation rule exists** for the coding-practices dimension (unlike CRA Art. 13(4) sentence 2 unified-risk-assessment carve-out for AI Act Art. 9 risk assessment, which does NOT extend to coding practices); verified by Art. 15(4) 6-category AI-attack-defence measures record review + Art. 15(4) proportionality calibration records (purpose + conditions of use) + QMS design-control record (Art. 17(1)(c) — design/design-control/design-verification) + Annex IV technical-documentation audit + Art. 9 risk-management-system documentation cross-reference + provider + MSA + AI Office inspection-ready evidence pack on request.

  priority: MUST
  verification_method: TEST
  dependencies: [7.2.1 (when AI-enabled CRA product — CRA Annex I Part I (2)(j)+(k) outcome-level obligations + AI Act Art. 15(4)–(5) AI-attack-defence categories stack vertically with no OJ-level consolidation rule; CRA-side outcomes + AI Act AI-attack-defence categories are complementary with non-substitution)]

  nist_csf: [PR.PS-06, ID.RA-01]

  applicable_if:
    regs: [AI_Act]
    scope_overlap: Conditional   # AI_Act↔CRA = Conditional Y on AI-enabled CRA products; AI_Act↔DORA = N typically (scope-disjoint in baseline); HL engages only on the Conditional Y integrated stack

  satisfaction:
    controls: []
    nodes: []

  considerations: |
    The AI Act secure-coding obligation is the high-risk AI system attack-defence mandate with 6 AI/ML-specific attack categories: data poisoning + model poisoning + adversarial examples + model evasion + confidentiality attacks + model flaws. For AI-enabled CRA products, AI Act Art. 15(4)–(5) AI-attack-defence stacks with CRA Annex I Part I (2)(j) + (2)(k) attack-surface + exploitation-mitigation — **no OJ-level consolidation rule exists** for the coding-practices dimension (unlike CRA Art. 13(4) sentence 2 unified-risk-assessment carve-out for AI Act Art. 9 risk assessment, which does NOT extend to coding practices).
```
